_README: Hand-authored STRUCTURE for the generated history (docs/history.html). The generator (tools/build-history.js) derives ALL dates, commit lists, ADRs and cart-birth data from git + index.json — this file only supplies what a parser can't infer: era boundaries, subsystem identity + matchers, importance tiers, the marked days, and the (mostly empty) editorial seam. Editorial prose, when written, follows docs/guides/voice.md. Re-run: `node tools/build-history.js`. Scope: weeks 1–6 (through 2026-07-03). NOTE: the `milestones` + `observations` snapshots are maintained through 2026-06-24; days after that carry eras + marked days + blurbs (a lighter timeline-extension pass) but no new milestones/observations yet.
title: dreamengine — a history
subtitle: How a fantasy console grew, week by week. Built structure-first from git, the STATUS ledger, and the decision log.

weeks.label: Week 3
weeks.tagline: MIDI, spatial audio — then roads, sims & a hardening pass

weeks.label: Week 4
weeks.tagline: Live instruments, a self-knowledge layer, the at-grade streets, a city that simulates itself — and the renderer taken down to the pixels

weeks.label: Week 5
weeks.tagline: A real city you can drive in 3D, the engine running on a phone, the day the tools turned on themselves — then a shared road grammar, a paint studio and a fleet-wide touch pass to close it out

weeks.label: Week 6
weeks.tagline: The instrument racks multiply, real multiplayer lands, and the studio grows a way out — to the Mac, the phone and the App Store

eras.title: Bootstrap — "get a window open"
eras.blurb: Ported from the sibling 'eventually' studio and renamed to dreamengine. One evening: editor chrome (Makefile, clipboard, tabs), assets embedded straight into the binary, a Windows cross-compile — capped at 23:55 by a single 'big batch' commit that drops the first sound system and the map editor.

eras.title: The API sprint
eras.blurb: A research braindump (Scratch / Strudel / Dilla) feeds two days of rapid-fire primitives and helpers. The cart pipeline forms (make-cart, tutorials, save/load), the web build lands (emscripten + a Build-for-Web button), and the first real synth arrives (ADSR / duty / LFO / resonant filter). The docs get reorganised by genre and the decision log is born.

eras.title: Rigor & tooling
eras.blurb: Less new surface, more correctness. Camera transforms, arc/ring primitives, and a hard rasterization-consistency push (one coverage test for circ/oval/tri/rrect). The debug harness lands — deterministic record/replay/script/trace. Fifty carts arrive at once via parallel Workflow batches. Geometry helpers, physics sketches, and fade() made immediate-mode.

eras.title: Subsystems are born
eras.blurb: The pivotal stretch. In two days: the profiler, dreamcad (a 3D model editor), held notes (note_on/off), modrack (a modular synth, steps 0→6, plus a module zoo), and the libtcc live backend (cart-as-script, de_state, hot-reload). The navkit instrument-port spec is written — it seeds the entire June sound saga — and mod-envelopes give the synth its second EG.

eras.title: Sound tools & the first stations
eras.blurb: The SFX editor and slider-driven generator land, plus drawable single-cycle waveforms (wave_set / INSTR_USER) and sample-accurate scheduling (schedule_hit). The first generative radio stations appear — bossa, ambient, jangle, lowend — and the cart taxonomy gets its capstones (the collision, movement and steering clusters). A dropped-request tripwire makes the sound queue trustworthy.

eras.title: Roland machines & the radio explosion
eras.blurb: The densest day yet. The Roland drum-machine family is synthesized from measured analog circuit values — CR-78, TR-808, TB-303, TR-909 — and a dozen radio stations pour out (city pop, dub, house, satie, tango, exotica, yacht, roadhouse, cocktail, ymo). radio.h is extracted as the shared station chassis, the first navkit port engines arrive (PLUCK, MALLET, FM), drive and echo become the first bus effects, and THE BAND panel + the improviser (improv.h) give the stations players.

eras.title: Mobile, widgets & the first keyboards
eras.blurb: A breather, then tooling. touch_ceiling() and the mobile worklist land, plus a debug overlay. ui.h ships a cross-input widget kit — button/slider/knob for mouse, touch and keyboard at once. The first keyboard instruments arrive (INSTR_ORGAN, INSTR_EPIANO) alongside teaching carts: flappy, the smallest complete game, and whatiscode, lesson zero.

eras.title: The engine-port marathon
eras.blurb: The navkit instrument ports land in a row until every modeled timbre exists: the envelope follower (a third modulation source), PD, REED (blown waveguide), PIPE (jet flute), GUITAR + PIANO (verbatim StifKarp), BOWED (Smith/McIntyre stick-slip), and finally BRASS — the last engine. Audio goes stereo (instrument_pan / LFO_PAN), the solo.h jam layer lets the player solo over a station, and the formant filter is specced.

eras.title: The effects cookbook & true colour
eras.blurb: The §8.10 effects roster completes: per-instrument chorus/flanger (effects on one instrument, not the whole mix), tape (wow/flutter/saturation), and the long-promised auto-wah — the 'scar' finally resolved as a bus effect. Drive modes and bitcrush ship too. On the graphics side, true-colour pixels arrive — pset_rgb / rectfill_rgb / pget_rgb — powering CPU shader carts.

eras.title: The mixing console & MIDI
eras.blurb: The engine becomes a real mixing console: a reorderable insert chain (fx_order), a multi-reverb tank pool, sidechain + bus compression (glue), a swept filter, and auto-pan. Then it learns to listen — engine-level MIDI keyboard input (CoreMIDI native, Web MIDI in the browser) plus keybed.h, a shared polyphonic keybed seven carts migrate onto. Audits (mono/poly, keycap) and the spline road world (roadnet) round out the stretch.

eras.title: Spatial audio
eras.blurb: Positional audio in two layers, one day. v1: a listener() + positioned voices give automatic pan, distance-volume and Doppler (the OpenAL model in 2D pixel space). v2: emitter buses move a whole effected source as one object, so its reverb/shimmer tail pans and Dopplers with it. The library also crosses its scale milestones — 14 MACRO engines, 32 simultaneous voices.

eras.title: Roads, stealth & engine tuning
eras.blurb: Attention turns to the big sims and the long tail of polish. The deterministic spline road world grows up — roadlab (lane-accurate roads + clothoid joints), interchange (a data-driven junction matrix), rampkit — and flank, a tactical stealth-squad game, takes real shape (fog of war, suppression, reaction time, an ammo economy). A street-level fill language starts underneath the roads (lotfill). On the audio side the waveguide top-octave tuning is finally fixed (PLUCK/REED/BRASS), a generalized all-engine tuner lands, and the first stability gates show up — level-check and fx-check, the start of a guard-rail suite.

eras.title: Junctions, jobs & guard-rails
eras.blurb: The longest day in the window, and a quiet turn in what the project is about. roadlab stops faking roads and becomes a real geometry engine — junctions described as a table of lane-links straight off the OpenDRIVE model, the trumpet/fork/triangle topology family, ring roads, loop and S-curve ramps, all generated from a type instead of placed by hand. Next to it a new kind of cart appears: thecut and handwork aren't games or instruments, they're production sims — what a worker costs, when it's worth hiring one, what an hour of your own hands is worth against a machine's. That's the second north star — deep honest sims under a lo-fi surface — made literal. And the whole engine grows guard-rails the same day: a suite of stability gates (effect-stacking, long-run soak, wasm-vs-native parity), the web pitch bug finally pinned and fixed (48k devices had been playing everything ~147 cents sharp), DC-blocked feedback loops, and a first real optimization pass on the fill rasterizer.

eras.title: Rebuilding the Moog, late
eras.blurb: A quiet late-night start to the week, all synth. moog is torn down and rebuilt as a proper 3-oscillator Model D with a real ladder filter (FILTER_LADDER), and onenote — a one-note funk groovebox — lands beside it. A null-result probe (pset draw-call batching, native + WebGL) gets captured rather than thrown away. The deepening that fills the next day starts here.

eras.title: One day, four fronts
eras.blurb: The single biggest day in the project's history — around 145 commits — and four threads advance at once, in parallel. (1) The synth grows up: oscillator hard sync, the Steiner-Parker multimode filter, and a flood of liveset playthings (euclid, turing, dubsiren, lpg, dubdesk, easel) and artist/genre radio stations (eno, plantasia, squarepusher fm, plaid fm, wba, the xx, afrobeat, vapor, lofi) — with ADR 0019 deciding the oscillators stay naive. (2) The project learns to read itself: cart-index.js and the TEACHES/LINEAGE convention, the spec() cart-logic harness, the ★ techniques compendium in the editor, teaches[] becoming a required index.json field behind a controlled-vocabulary gate, plus cart-dupes.js and teaching-gaps.md. (3) The at-grade streets: streetlab runs M1→M7 and a lane-section refactor — curb returns, T-junctions, turn lanes, the street web, sidewalks/crosswalks, the mini-roundabout, the typed OpenDRIVE cross-section, bike lanes and bulb-outs — the at-grade sibling of roadlab, spec-locked. (4) The turn toward a larger game: the clip+reel pipeline (clips carry audio; compose-clips stitches reels), the showreel-teaser note, and a wave of game carts — facegen (procedural street portraits + conversation), runecrawl (circuit-dungeon), beltworks (Factorio-lite), boom — plus cards.h, cursor.h and the 04b-mouse tutorial.

eras.title: The city wakes up
eras.blurb: It opens at midnight like a closing — boom ships as an explosion-and-fire sandbox, the week's carts go live in one big site publish (boom, 04b-mouse, beltworks, facegen, logic, runecrawl, streetlab), streetlab and facegen get end-of-session resume notes. Then the day turns into the opposite of a closing: three city sims wake up at once and run all day. gridcity is a SimCity in miniature — a data-layer sim where land value, crime, pollution, power, water and fire feed back on each other, grown from a v1 to a real RCI-demand city in an afternoon. cityplan merges lotfill and building interiors into one procedural town under a zoom-banded roof lift. trackgen's cars stop racing and start living — traffic mode, jams, a light, collisions that express each driver's mass. boom keeps growing teeth: destructible walls, ram cars, pedestrians, dithered smoke. And streetlab reaches its Stage-2 superblock with a circuity metric. A whole world to drive through, built between one midnight and the next.

eras.title: Down to the pixels
eras.blurb: The day the renderer got interrogated. The big one: the software-canvas investigation finally RUNS, and comes back GO — rasterize the whole frame on the CPU into a byte-identical framebuffer and cityplan drops from 11.4ms to 5.0ms a frame. Phases 0 through 2 land in a row (cbuf + UpdateTexture, then spr/print/tritex/span-fills as CPU blits, then camera zoom in software with rotation falling back to the GPU), and the win is honestly scoped — it's a pset-bound win, not a universal one. Underneath it, a determinism question gets chased to the floor: the det-probes prove rotated fills, strokes and sprites are gap-free and reproducible, RotSprite (EPX ×3) goes head-to-head and wins on lines but not lone specks, and cross-arch libm trig drift gets quantized away. The roads got a big chunk of their own. Field-based road rendering — the long-queued refactor that draws a road from a per-pixel lateral-coverage field instead of stamped quads — finally lands, shipped opt-in behind the road-check oracle (framebuffer invariants: no naked edges, no strays, no floating kerb at any angle) and blessed by ADR 0021 (the geometry lives in plain 2D; the pseudo-3D view is just a rendering adapter). cityview puts a GTA1-ish four-mode camera over the world and folds the overpass raised-flyover experiment into itself, and trackgen's traffic learns cross-roads, right-of-way reservations, K-turns and police chases.

eras.title: The city, loaded for real
eras.blurb: The roads stopped being a sandbox and became a place. roadview learns a packed binary format (.rvb) so tens of megabytes of OpenStreetMap load in under a second, its pools grow to fit ALL of Amsterdam (~4.2M points / 575k ways), and it stops sampling and pulls the FULL ladder of the data — every road class, hashed understory, multipolygon water, and SimCity-style zoning layers (landuse / rail / sand) with outlined building footprints. An absolute zoom ceiling lets a whole city zoom down to street detail. The cart loads its world at runtime, so you swap cities by swapping a file — no regen — and cityview gets published to the gallery. The groundwork for driving a real city.

eras.title: It runs on the phone
eras.blurb: The iOS port crossed from spikes to the real thing. Phase 2 lands: the actual studio.c + sound.h — the real engine, zero Raylib via the DE_NO_RAYLIB seam — renders omnichord pixel-correct and sounds it through CoreAudio on a real iPhone, UIKit touch driving it. The AUv3 extension stops hosting a stand-in and hosts the REAL engine as a sample-clocked sequencer, then takes host MIDI so it's a playable instrument rack inside other apps. The load-bearing renderer question gets settled and written down — ADR-0024: the software canvas is canonical for 2D (no ANGLE on iOS); 3D stays GPU-only and off the initial list — after on-device FPS is measured (2D carts hold 59–60fps; tritex ~10fps). A perf harness logs frames to the Documents dir. Nothing left to set up; device work just runs here now.

eras.title: The tools turn on themselves
eras.blurb: A multi-track day whose headline was the project turning its tools on itself. The knowledge layer: build-context gathers one cart's scattered docs, its horizontal twin topic-brief assembles a feature dossier across the whole repo, and lint-xrefs finds the docs that should cross-link but don't (432 missing backlinks → 42 once it learns to spot domain hubs by in-degree). Then the views — design-board reads every design doc's STATUS line into a lifecycle board (the ★ todo page, styled as a lo-fi Comic-Mono console checklist), and build-reflections turns the field-notes journal into the ★ reflections page beside it, both over a shared doc-status.js that owns the phase vocabulary. The rest of the day is honesty passes against those views: triage 29 unmarked design docs, relocate 5 misplaced field-notes, migrate carts' hidden prose todos into de:meta.todo[], wire the 260-cart-todo backlog onto the board, and — the closing move — audit all 15 field notes against the repo and record on each what it actually became (the journal flips from 10 observed / 0 incorporated to 10 incorporated). In parallel: citydrive turns the loaded city into a drivable pseudo-3D world — real metre-scaled car, mouse orbit/zoom, terrain elevation off a DEM, water and mountains around the edges, a pitched perspective camera — and a '📱 to iPhone' button lands in the editor to deploy the live cart to a connected device.

eras.title: A road grammar, a paint studio, and touch everywhere
eras.blurb: The last day of the week runs on four fronts at once. The roads get a shared grammar: roadkit.h is declared GO — connectivity FIRST, markings later — and citydrive becomes its first consumer, rendering Delft as one connected asphalt surface with OSM-driven lane markings and carriageway width, on-road cycle lanes (fietsstrook), canals drawn under the roads, raised bridge decks and dashed tunnels. sloop starts actually driving the real city — P1 rungs A→C wire road_at() over a real-Delft spatial index, render the OSM world, let the surface drive handling, and crash into real buildings; drag a .rvb and you drive a real place. squishy turns into a painting studio — a SELECT tool grows through three stages into a per-stroke vector editor (pick → highlight → edit properties), joined by drip / calligraphy / oil / rotation brushes, a per-stroke outline and a bevel 'sun' you can angle, colour and spin, two boil styles, CMY colours and z-order. And touch controls generalize to N action buttons (an A/B/X/Y diamond, 0–4) with TOUCH_DPAD4/DPAD8 move modes, a scale-to-fit band and a debug introspection API — then get swept across ~16 existing carts in a single pass (boids, doomfire, defender, galaga, vampire, rogue, drmario, columns, puyo and more). Underneath: the Track-A teaching carts (23-basics, 24-building-blocks, 25-patterns, 10b-camera, 06b-chords), filterenv's MONO legato Model-D voice, RAILS window-fill sizing, and stale-doc-check.js.

eras.title: Instrument racks, real multiplayer, and 3D back on the phone
eras.blurb: A monster day on three fronts. Music: a wave of instrument carts ships. acidrack is the RB-338 rack — two 303s plus a real 909 and curated 808, an FX strip, per-device effects, per-voice TUNE/DEC/CHAR knobs, and a seeded generator with a song code and WAV export. tracker is a Song→Chain→Phrase tracker in the LSDJ/M8 lineage (four tracks, mnemonic FX riding note_*, a random-song generator). yachtrack is the SESSION DESK that opens the yacht radio into an editable rack — tinyjam rack #2, the first chord-first rack and the first radio→rack seed handoff, locked to a 44-pair golden corpus. pocketbox is a buttons-only PGB-1 groovebox (p-locks, ratchets, conditions, a chord track, a ROLL-a-groove generator). delia is a concrete tape-kit that becomes the Radiophonic Workshop's increment 0, and otafamily is four otamatones over a gesture live-loop. The engine gains FILTER_DIODE — the real TB-303 diode-ladder squelch, with filter-spec.js as its acceptance oracle. Systems: multiplayer arrives for real — lockstep over UDP (rung 1) grows to LAN-by-IP, an engine-owned Host/Join/Solo boot lobby, LAN auto-discovery and a Winsock (.exe) port, all gated behind de_players() so solo carts stay clean — and the editor grows send-to-a-friend exports (.exe, a signed+notarized Mac .app via the new mac-app.sh, deploy-to-iPhone). And 3D comes back to the phone: sw_tritex gets a scan-box clamp and hoisted plot (infiniminer 1204→3.4ms, 358×), which on re-measurement overturns ADR-0024's no-3D-on-iOS call (podracer 89→6ms, infiniminer 10.9ms @59fps on an A13). Plus sloop's car↔foot seam (get out, walk, step into a house and the floor plan is real) and 26-first3d, the whole 3D pipeline on one spinning textured quad.

eras.title: The studio learns to ship
eras.blurb: The day the project grew a way out to the store. The engine seam lands first: de_switch_cart() gives every cart its own sound, video and sprite-sheet context — a config-request log recorded at drain and replayed on switch — so two carts run in one binary with zero edits, proven by the bundle-spike and hardened by ADR-0027 (cart-facing sound state flows ONLY through the request queue, the rule a bpm tempo-jump regression proved). On top of it, build-app.js turns an apps/<name>/app.json manifest into a multi-cart binary — a four-rung ladder from the dispatcher shim to a de:meta-fed launcher cart (tinyjam-menu) to a signed+notarized .app — and the same manifest builds and runs on iOS, in-app purchases and all (Spikes A + B). The umbrella app gets its name: Tiny Jam. Around the build sits a whole store pipeline, script-prepares-then-agent-decides by design (store-agents.md, over ADR-0026's in-house App Store Connect plan): the ASO lab — aso-research, aso-suggest, aso-lint, aso-compose, aso-brief and its aso-coverage mirror, and aso-score with a winnability check — plus store-shots (native frames → exact device screenshots), store-contact (a hero-frame contact sheet) and press-kit (a self-contained press page). The editor grows an Apps view that drives all of it. Two more threads open the same day: lay.h graduates from a layout vocabulary to a runtime library header and the device-adaptive-layout project kicks off (Phase 0 proven by rackfit's finger-sized reflow, Phase 1a's runtime canvas-dim globals landed), while build-app-reel.js and an in-editor trailer builder start turning a manifest into a demand-generating trailer.

eras.title: Carts that fit any glass, and a trailer to sell them
eras.blurb: Device-adaptive layout gets real. The framebuffer moves to the heap with a runtime stride and grows on demand (byte-identical to the old fixed path, so a de:meta resizable cart reflows to any size) — the editor ▶-run reflows it live, a harness --resize sweep exercises it, and Phase 2 lands iOS carts filling the device with safe-area insets and allowed rotation. Alongside it the in-editor trailer builder takes shape: per-clip trim + speed with a live visual preview, a drag-to-arrange timeline (add/remove/reorder/repeat), spec text cards with a magic-colour overlay grammar and a .reel @card syntax, an entrance vocabulary, a seamless loop-close transition, and beat-sync (breathe punches, boil ticks on the beat) — with the titlecard cart as its kinetic pixel-type slice 1 (boil + breathe, a full in/hold/out timeline with per-end effects). Underneath: raylib_compat stubs the window-size calls (GetScreenWidth/Height, SetWindowSize, ImageCrop), a DE_SHOW_SIZE overlay reads out live W×H, and welcome gets a leaner starter template and a new tagline.

eras.title: Record, replay, and the first lockstep handshake
eras.blurb: The editor learns to record and replay input tracks (.rec) — and the information-architecture seam it surfaces (where recorded media lives) opens the editor-media lane. The responsive work grows a gate: ui-audit gains a --resize sweep and a panel×size matrix, catches off-screen and overlapping widgets (not just text), and acidrack becomes the first rack to reflow (Phase 3 — landscape tabs, wrapping master knobs, content-aware tab thresholds). Netplay rung 5a begins and reaches code-complete in a day: the lockstep core compiles on web (a DE_NET_CORE/DE_NET_BUILD split, a transport seam, a non-blocking barrier and an echo fake-peer), then a WebSocket transport and a zero-dep relay — browser netplay code-complete. Around it the instrument museum map (the hub over every instrument doc) and a mic-and-sampling sketch get written, the design-system companion pages trace the evolution trail, and resolution-portable input tracks are reframed as an attract-mode IAP storefront.

eras.title: Multiplayer goes live, the repo splits, and a world draws its own roads
eras.blurb: The biggest single day of the week runs on four fronts. Multiplayer goes live — rung 5a is verified peer-to-peer, a Render relay is deployed and wss-verified, rooms are cart-scoped, and the site grows a play-together button and a room bar; a WebRTC P2P spike (rung 5b) then reaches Mac↔iPhone at ~12ms over wifi. The repo splits in two: site/ is untracked and the published gallery moves to its own public repo, this engine becomes dreamengine-studio, and publish-cart.sh grows two legs. The store lane pushes to TestFlight — Tiny Jam's bundle id is reserved on ASC, per-app icons stage through the manifest, and testflight.sh archives and uploads (v0.1 build live on ASC), with the iOS-26 SDK and SKTestSession wobbles logged. And a world starts generating itself: the worldgen ladder lands rungs 0–3 in one push — sndi-check (the street-network metric oracle) at rung 0, roadnet2's unified road_at() graph query (rung 1a) extracted to worldnet.h as the shared world spine (rung 1) the moment sloop became its second consumer, then citygrow's population-density field (rung 2) and per-city tensor-field arterials (rung 3), with the first generated-vs-real SNDi numbers measured. Plus the tr-606 drumatix, completing the Roland wing.

eras.title: Instruments reflow, a scale grid you can play, and the studio learns to promote
eras.blurb: The 101-commit day, the largest of the week. Instrument UI learns to reflow: a device-matrix reference is written, and acidwire flips acidrack's layout through the whole device matrix with one key — rendering the real safe-area (notch/island/home-bar keep-outs), a real 303 step editor, per-instrument mute and patterns, a focus/fullscreen state and a desktop-fake-flipper-vs-device dual mode — while epianofit mocks the keybed's responsive layout, and the responsive-instrument-ui guide plus ADR-0028 (sensible defaults, optional tweaks) codify the playbook. Three instrument carts ship: scalegrid, a scale-locked isomorphic pad grid you actually play (11 scales, an OCT↔4TH row offset, SQR↔HEX Tonnetz packing with a nearest-centre hit-test, and VOICE and SIZE cycles, all pinned by a 71-assertion spec); newworld, a Joe Meek 'I Hear a New World' found-sound machine; and circlemachine, Raymond Scott's Circle Machine ring-sequencer. The studio learns to promote: asc-push.js pushes metadata/screenshots/IAPs to App Store Connect and marks promoted purchases (with a Store.swift PurchaseIntent), the leads marketeer maps a cart to its tribes and the venues where they gather (music coverage tuned to ~90%), and the editor's information architecture resolves into Make / Promote / Ship — a new per-cart Promote tab, with Dream as the zeroth verb closing the loop to a cycle. Netplay rung 5b (WebRTC P2P) is built, play-tested and published.

markedDays.2026-05-28: The bootstrap night — a whole studio stood up in one evening, from initial commit to a midnight 'big batch'.
markedDays.2026-06-02: The big bang — modrack AND the libtcc live backend both born on the same day, with held-notes and dreamcad alongside.
markedDays.2026-06-05: The radio explosion — a dozen stations and the whole Roland drum-machine family born in one day, plus radio.h and the first port engines.
markedDays.2026-06-10: The engine program finishes — INSTR_BRASS lands as the last modeled timbre, so every instrument the library needs now exists.
markedDays.2026-06-12: The mixing console day — the effects bus becomes reorderable (fx_order) with multi-reverb and sidechain, and MIDI keyboards play the engine (CoreMIDI + Web MIDI + keybed.h).
markedDays.2026-06-15: Spatial audio — listener + positioned voices, then whole emitter buses that move (Doppler + a shimmer tail that travels with the source), shipped in one day.
markedDays.2026-06-16: The roads-and-tuning surge — roadlab, interchange and rampkit plus the flank stealth game in one push, the waveguide top-octave tuning finally fixed, and the first audio stability gates (level-check, fx-check) land.
markedDays.2026-06-17: The day the sims got serious — roadlab becomes a real OpenDRIVE-style junction engine, the first production/economy carts (thecut, handwork) make the deep-sim north star literal, and the engine grows a full guard-rail suite plus the web pitch fix.
markedDays.2026-06-22: The long day — around 145 commits, the biggest in the project's history. Four threads advance in parallel: the synth deepens (hard sync, Steiner-Parker, a flood of liveset playthings and artist stations), the project builds tools to read itself (cart-index, the spec() harness, the ★ techniques compendium, the teaches/lineage convention), streetlab grows a full at-grade street network (M1→M7 + lane-section refactor), and the turn toward a larger game begins (the clip/reel pipeline, facegen, runecrawl, beltworks).
markedDays.2026-06-23: The city day — three city sims wake up at once. gridcity is a SimCity in miniature (land value, crime, pollution, power, water, fire, RCI demand — born and grown to v1.5 in one afternoon), cityplan merges procedural lots and building interiors into one town, and trackgen's cars stop racing and start living (traffic, jams, a light, mass-aware collisions). boom matures into a destructible-physics sandbox and streetlab reaches its Stage-2 superblock — all on top of a midnight site publish that closes out week 3.
markedDays.2026-06-24: Down to the pixels — the software-canvas investigation runs and comes back GO: a CPU framebuffer renderer, byte-identical to the GPU path, drops cityplan from 11.4ms to 5.0ms a frame (Phases 0–2). Alongside it the det-probes pin rotation determinism to the floor (gap-free, cross-arch trig quantized, RotSprite benchmarked), field-based road rendering ships behind the road-check oracle (ADR 0021), and trackgen's traffic learns crossings, right-of-way and police chases.
markedDays.2026-07-01: The week closes on four fronts — roadkit.h declared GO with citydrive as its first consumer (one connected OSM road surface: markings, cycle lanes, canals-under-roads, bridge decks, tunnels), sloop driving real Delft (P1 rungs A–C), squishy grown into a per-stroke vector paint studio, and touch controls generalized to N buttons then swept across ~16 carts.
markedDays.2026-07-02: The racks-and-net day — a wave of instrument carts (acidrack's RB-338 rack, tracker, yachtrack rack #2, pocketbox, delia, otafamily), the real TB-303 diode ladder (FILTER_DIODE), lockstep multiplayer over UDP→LAN with send-to-a-friend exports and mac-app.sh, and sw_tritex's 358× win that puts 3D back on the phone (overturning ADR-0024).
markedDays.2026-07-03: The day the studio learned to ship — de_switch_cart runs two carts in one binary (ADR-0027), build-app.js turns a manifest into a multi-cart app that builds signed for Mac and runs on iOS with in-app purchases (named Tiny Jam), a whole ASO/store/press pipeline lands with an editor Apps view, and lay.h graduates as the device-adaptive-layout project begins.
markedDays.2026-07-04: Carts that fit any glass — the framebuffer grows on demand so a resizable cart reflows to any size (iOS fill + safe-area + rotation), and the in-editor trailer builder takes shape (timeline, spec text cards, beat-sync) with titlecard as slice 1.
markedDays.2026-07-05: Record, replay, and the first lockstep handshake — the editor records/replays input tracks (.rec), ui-audit gains the responsive-layout gate, and netplay rung 5a reaches browser code-complete (lockstep core on web → WebSocket transport → zero-dep relay).
markedDays.2026-07-06: The biggest day of the week, on four fronts — multiplayer goes live (Render relay, cart-scoped rooms, a WebRTC 5b spike at ~12ms), the repo splits into dreamengine-studio, the store lane uploads Tiny Jam v0.1 to TestFlight, and the worldgen ladder lands rungs 0–3 (sndi-check, worldnet.h, citygrow's density field + tensor arterials).
markedDays.2026-07-07: The 101-commit day — instruments learn to reflow (acidwire through the whole device matrix, epianofit, the responsive-instrument-ui playbook, ADR-0028), three instrument carts ship (scalegrid's playable scale grid with a 71-assertion spec, newworld, circlemachine), and the studio learns to promote (asc-push, the leads marketeer, the editor's Make/Promote/Ship IA).

threads._README: RESEARCH THREADS — topics that spawned a trail of docs, handoffs and carts. Declared by topic (like `subsystems`): the generator auto-collects every doc/ADR whose filename matches a `match` term, splits them into notes/handoffs/decisions, unions the carts that cite them (via the strong-signal scan), and computes the date span. AUTOMATIC: a new matching doc/handoff/cart folds in on the next `node tools/build-history.js` with no editing. The only hand-authored part is `title` + the voiced `blurb` (follows guides/voice.md); leave `blurb` empty and a structural sentence is derived instead.

items.title: Roads & a world to drive on
items.blurb: This one started small and would not stop. The idea was just to vary procplaces' roads-and-cities generator — split density into land-use and intensity, kill the grid look. Then it hit the wall: arced roads. You can't bend a road on a global grid without everything downstream quietly lying about where it goes. So roadnet threw the grid out for a node-lattice of splines — local, deterministic, infinite, honest. That cracked it open, and then it just kept going: a street level under the map (the fill language in lotfill, then cityplan's whole procedural town), real junctions (interchange), the at-grade streets (streetlab, all the way to a typed OpenDRIVE cross-section), the traffic that actually lives on them (trackgen — lane-keeping, crossings, right-of-way, even police chases). And sloop — the cart that actually drives the thing — is the whole reason it had to be true and not faked. The latest turn went philosophical: ADR 0021 says the road geometry lives in plain 2D, the pseudo-3D view is just an adapter, and the field-based road renderer proves it. Many handoffs deep, still open. The longest thread in the project, and the one most likely to eat another month.

items.title: The instrument-engine ports
items.blurb: Almost every voice in here is a port of navkit, done one at a time until the whole modeled orchestra existed — pluck, reed, the jet-flute pipe, guitar and piano straight off StifKarp, the bowed string, and brass last. The rule we learned the hard way: port the oscillator verbatim, don't "improve" it on the way in — every shortcut showed up later as a tuning scar (the waveguides go flat at the top of the keyboard; still chasing that one). Four handoffs and a dedicated tuner cart are the receipts. Trust your ears over the label — the doc says Rhodes, the ear says no, the ear wins.

items.title: The effects bus
items.blurb: The engine quietly turned into a mixing console. It started as 'effects are recipes, not primitives' — don't ship a reverb knob, ship the settings that make a space — and then the bus grew teeth: drive, echo, tape, the auto-wah that got built wrong once and torn out and redone right. The big call was making effects per-instrument and reorderable instead of one master strip, so a chorus can sit on the Rhodes alone and the order of the chain is yours. The boutique-pedals roadmap is the wishlist that keeps feeding it.

items.title: The radio stations
items.blurb: The stations are the part that plays itself. Each one is a genre studied blind first — write the ideal band from the music, before reading how the last cart did it, so the library doesn't homogenize — then built: a seeded song, a chord brain, voices picked to match. The blind-briefs are that first honest sketch; the genre-fidelity ledger is the brutal follow-up that says where we faked an instrument or skipped a brain. Bossa, dub, house, satie, a dozen more. The dream underneath is tinyjam racks — radios as song generators you can actually edit.

items.title: Mobile & touch
items.blurb: A desktop toy meeting a phone, and the phone winning a lot of the arguments. Real device testing turned up the stuff you can't reason about from a chair — the five-touch iPhone ceiling, phantom touches, fingers that swipe-tap-do-five-things-and-crash-it. Out of that came the loupe (a magnifier so you can actually grab a tiny knob), the shared widget kit so every cart handles mouse, touch and keyboard at once, and a couple of audits that swept every cart for whether a phone could even play it. Not glamorous. Load-bearing.

items.title: Spatial audio
items.blurb: Sound in space, done in two passes in basically a day. First the OpenAL move: a listener, voices with positions, automatic pan and distance-volume and Doppler in flat 2D pixel space. Then the better idea — move a whole effected source as one object, so its reverb tail and shimmer travel with it instead of staying nailed to the center. Small thread, but it's the one that makes a passing car sound like a passing car.

items.title: Making work into a game
items.blurb: Somewhere in here a second project woke up inside the first one. Not a game and not an instrument — a sim about work itself: what a worker costs, when it's worth hiring one, what your own two hands are worth against a machine that never gets tired. thecut and handwork are the first probes, the sloop-production notes are the thinking out loud. It's the second north star written down plainly — deep honest sims under a lo-fi surface — and it's the part most likely to quietly become the main thing.

items.title: Making it fast without breaking it
items.blurb: Late in the window the question stopped being 'what can it do' and started being 'how cheap is it.' A fleet-wide CPU survey, a stress cart that does nothing but hammer the fill rasterizer, and a first real optimization — solid spans collapsed into a single rectangle instead of a pixel at a time — with the old path kept right beside it so you can prove the new one matches. Then the big swing: batching draw calls didn't pan out (a captured null result), so instead of fighting the GPU, skip it — rasterize the whole frame on the CPU into one framebuffer and blit it once. The software-canvas probe ran the experiment for real and came back GO: byte-identical to the GPU path, cityplan from 11.4ms to 5.0ms a frame. Honest about the limit, though — it's a win for pset-bound carts, not everything. And going to the CPU forced a harder question the GPU hid: is rotation even deterministic across machines? The det-probes chased that to the floor — gap-free rotated fills, cross-arch libm trig quantized, RotSprite benchmarked head-to-head. The rule the guide writes down: optimize in a loop you can't fool — measure, change one thing, measure again, keep the A/B.

decisionNotes._README: Voiced one-liners for the 'Roads not taken' cards (and any decision worth a readable line), following guides/voice.md — replaces the dry ADR context paragraph on the page. Keyed by ADR basename without .md. No entry = fall back to the ADR's first-paragraph summary.
decisionNotes.0001-cut-coroutine-process-model: The DIV process model — the thing we'd put on the box as what PICO-8 doesn't have — got cut in week one. A coroutine per game object sounds great until you're writing the C-side scheduler instead of the game. Killed it before it could set the shape of everything after it.
decisionNotes.0008-cut-turtle-graphics-api: Shipped a whole Logo turtle, nine functions, then went looking for who used it. One cart. Out it came — a feature with one user isn't a feature, it's a maintenance bill.
decisionNotes.0013-cut-music-api: music() was the PICO-8 half of the sound plan: pattern banks, four channels, the works. Nobody ever called it — we'd already picked code-first music twice over. This is the exact fork where sound stopped being a tracker and started becoming a synth.
decisionNotes.0014-cut-unused-convenience-helpers: Three convenience helpers, zero carts using them, each the less-loved sibling of one that caught on. The usage audit found them; we cut them. Convenience nobody reaches for is just more surface to keep working.

observations._README: Cross-week RETROSPECTS — conclusions that only surface from reading the whole arc back at once, NOT legible commit-by-commit. Distinct from each era's `editorial` seam (which annotates one era from inside it); these look down on the entire window. Each `evidence` line is grounded in a number the generator can re-derive from git/index.json, so a future refresh can re-check it. Refresh by re-running the `recipe` against the current window and rewriting `items` + bumping `asOf`.
observations.title: Retrospect — reading the whole arc back
observations.recipe: How these were got, kept so it can be done again in due time: after the history page + spine are written, read them BACK as a finished whole — the rendered docs/history.html (which now mines design-doc CONTENT automatically: the per-era 'design-note spotlight' pulls each doc's friendly one-liner from docs/README.md + the carts that cite it, and 'Roads not taken' surfaces the cut ADRs), this spine, and the raw counts (`git log --since=<from> --date=short --pretty=%ad | sort | uniq -c` for per-day density; `grep -o '"subsystem": "[a-z0-9]*"' history-spine.json | sort | uniq -c` for the milestone mix; cart/ADR totals). Then ask the question that started it: "since we've written the docs/history code and text, can you now read it back and come to insights/conclusions you couldn't before?" Mine the negative space too — STATUS.md's 'Decided-against' ledger + the cut-* ADRs + the handoff docs (where work got parked) are as telling as what shipped. This refresh also reads the two present-tense sources the page now renders as the FRONTIER band (a capstone beside the retrospect — the retrospect looks back, the frontier shows the open edge NOW): docs/HANDOFF.md's active lanes (via `node tools/handoff.js --json`) and the polish debt from `node tools/cart-todos.js --count` (surfaced as a NUMBER, never the list). A landed lane simply drops off — so the frontier is deliberately NOT woven into any dated past era. Rule: every claim cites a derived number, so it stays honest with the repo like the rest of the page. Caveat learned: per-day commit counts shift with timezone/day-boundary choices — cross-check before calling any single day a peak or a lull (the apparent '06-13 lull' was a date-grouping artifact).

items.title: It became a synthesizer with a screen
items.body: The stated identity is a learn-C fantasy console in the PICO-8 lineage. The revealed identity, in aggregate, is a software studio that happens to render graphics: 14 modeled instrument engines, a reorderable effects bus, MIDI, and positional audio. Each synth commit reads as 'just another cart' alone — only the pile shows sound ate the project. And this isn't only emergent: VISION.md and design/second-north-star.md name the shift outright ('a second pursuit has grown up alongside it… it now drives much of the work' — deep honest sims behind a lo-fi surface), so the git aggregate is confirming a turn the project already saw in itself.
items.evidence: 58 of 174 milestones are audio (36 sound + 7 effects + 7 radio + 7 machines + 1 modrack ≈ 33%) — a clear majority through week 3 (38 of 60 ≈ 63%), but from week 4 on audio is no longer the largest thread and the share keeps sliding as roads (now 24, the 2nd-largest subsystem), iOS and the store/promote threads grow (see observations 13/14 and 16–19); the pivot is documented in design/second-north-star.md (REFLECTION, 2026-06-09).

items.title: The fantasy console kept amputating its own fantasy-console features
items.body: The page celebrates what shipped, but the negative space is just as revealing: the project repeatedly removed PICO-8/DIV-style conveniences to keep the from-scratch lesson honest and code-first. The DIV coroutine/process model — billed in VISION as the headline differentiator from PICO-8 — was cut in WEEK ONE. Turtle graphics shipped, then was removed for one user. The music() pattern-bank was cut because the project had chosen code-first music twice over — which is the same decision that sent audio down the synthesis road (observation 1). Three zero-adoption helpers went after an API-usage audit. The throughline: subtract anything that lets the learner skip the real thing.
items.evidence: Four cut-ADRs: 0001 (coroutine model, cut 2026-05-30, week 1), 0008 (turtle graphics removed 2026-06-01), 0013 (music() → code-first, 2026-06-04), 0014 (3 unused helpers, 2026-06-04); plus STATUS.md's 'Decided-against / deferred' ledger.

items.title: One spec set three weeks' trajectory
items.body: The navkit instrument-port spec, written on 06-02 in the 'subsystems born' era, is the keystone. Trace it forward: the spec → the engine-port marathon (PLUCK→BRASS) → which created the demand for effects (all those voices need processing) → which created the mixing console (they need routing) → which created spatial audio (they need placing). Most of the last two weeks is downstream of one week-1 planning artifact, not one feature.
items.evidence: Spine flags it 'seeds the entire June sound saga'; the engine milestones it spawned span 06-05 (PLUCK) through 06-10 (BRASS, 'the last engine').

items.title: Throughput compounded — it never plateaued
items.body: The week-1 tooling (harness, profiler, libtcc live backend, make-cart + parallel Workflow batches, the radio.h/ui.h/keybed.h chassis) was the real product: weeks 2–3 are almost pure content riding that scaffolding. The marked-day note calls 06-05 'the densest day yet' — true when written, but it's since been beaten many times over, and the all-time peak keeps moving: 06-22 held it at ~150 for a week, then 06-30 broke it at 160 — and the window keeps clearing 100 to the end (07-01/02/03 all over 116, then a lighter mid-week-6 tail before a 101-commit finale on 07-07). Those late peaks rode the original scaffolding AND each new layer built on top of it (the self-knowledge tooling of observation 9, then the multi-cart app pipeline). The investment never stopped paying off as accelerating output.
items.evidence: Per-day commits (git --date=short): week 1 runs 18–60; weeks 2–3 routinely break 100 (138, 129, 118, 113); the top five are 06-30=160, 06-22=150, 06-10=138, 07-03=136, 06-04=129, with 07-02=128, 07-01=116 and the window's final day 07-07=101 (tied with 06-24) close behind. 06-05 = 106. 448 carts total.

items.title: Every shared header is crystallized duplication — always in that order
items.body: The library headers under runtime/ never lead; they trail. radio.h was extracted after a dozen stations; keybed.h after seven keyboard carts; ui.h after hand-rolled widgets; pointer.h was retrofitted across the remaining hand-rolled-pool carts. ADR-0006 makes this deliberate — the engine doesn't own these, they precipitate out of cart-land once the pain is felt. Abstraction trails repetition every time.
items.evidence: radio.h 'extracted as the shared station chassis' (06-05); pointer.h 'adopt across the 13 remaining hand-rolled-pool carts' (06-13); keybed.h 'seven carts migrate onto' (console-midi era).

items.title: The audio system recapitulated the history of studio gear
items.body: It isn't one 'sound' blob — it's four strict layers, each presupposing the last: synthesis (instruments exist) → effects (instruments get processed) → console (instruments get mixed/routed/reordered) → spatial (instruments get placed in space). You can't have a mixing-console day until 14 engines and bus effects exist to mix; you can't place a signal until there's one worth placing. The order was emergent, and it matches how real studios evolved.
items.evidence: Era sequence: engine-marathon (synthesis) → fx-truecolor (effects) → console-midi (console) → spatial (placement), 06-08 through 06-15.

items.title: Long unresolved threads are only visible in aggregate
items.body: A single commit hides the threads that run across weeks. The auto-wah is described as 'the scar finally resolved as a bus effect' — implying an earlier parked attempt that sat unbuilt while a cheaper per-voice stand-in held the place. The engine marathon is the same shape: a goal declared early ('until every modeled timbre exists') and closed out only at BRASS. The whole-arc view is what surfaces these.
items.evidence: audio-notes.md still names a 'PARKED §8.10 bus wah' beside the per-voice cousin; auto-wah milestone resolves it in the fx-truecolor era (06-11).

items.title: A second project woke up inside the first
items.body: For three weeks the revealed identity was 'a synthesizer with a screen' (observation 1). The tail of the window adds a second current that isn't sound at all: production and economy sims — thecut, handwork — where the subject is labour, cost and delegation, not graphics or audio. It reads as the second-north-star reflection (06-09) finally cashed out into carts rather than prose: deep honest sims under a lo-fi surface. Too young to call a third pillar at week 3's end, but it's the first work in the window that's neither a game, an instrument, nor a tool — a new category, and the one the project then seemed most curious about. Week 4 acts on that curiosity (beltworks, a factory economy) and starts wiring it into a bigger whole — see observation 10.
items.evidence: Production/economy-sim carts: thecut + handwork (06-17, citing design/sloop-production.md + design/second-north-star.md, REFLECTION 2026-06-09), then beltworks (06-22, a Factorio-lite factory builder) — the window's only carts whose subject is economy/labour rather than graphics or sound.

items.title: The project built a mirror to read its own corpus
items.body: Three weeks of accumulation produced ~400 carts; week 4 is when the project turned around and built the machinery to UNDERSTAND that pile. Almost all in one day: cart-index.js (a computed 'what teaches what' index), the spec() cart-logic harness (deterministic regression tests for game logic — the gameplay twin of week 3's audio gates), the ★ techniques compendium in the editor, the teaches/lineage convention promoted to a required, controlled-vocabulary index.json field, cart-dupes.js (cross-cart duplication → refactor candidates), and teaching-gaps.md (what the API offers but no tutorial teaches). This is a new KIND of work — not engine, not content, not even the guard-rails of week 3 (which verify correctness); it makes the corpus legible to itself. And it obeys the same law as observation 5: you only build a technique index once there are ~400 techniques to index. The mirror trails the corpus, exactly as abstraction trails repetition.
items.evidence: A new 'introspect' subsystem debuts at 5 milestones in a single day (2026-06-22), and 06-30's self-knowledge day (observation 15) grows it to 9 — now tied for 4th-largest of 25 subsystems (with gfx); cart-index.js / spec.js / cart-dupes.js / the compendium / the teaches[] hard-gate all born 06-22; 448 carts in index.json.

items.title: The carts began assembling into one game
items.body: Through week 3 the carts were standalone — an instrument, a toy, a sim, each complete in itself. Week 4 is the first time they're treated as PARTS of a larger whole. A showreel/teaser design note is written 'for the larger game', a big-game backlog enumerates per-cart remaining work plus the cross-cutting seams, and a clip+reel pipeline is built specifically to show the assembled thing (clips now carry audio; compose-clips stitches them into a reel with transitions). Underneath, the pieces converge: streetlab draws the streets, beltworks the factory economy, facegen the people (procedural street portraits + a conversation system), with interiors and street-level content briefs behind them. This is observation 8's 'second project' growing past sims-as-experiments into a game-in-assembly — roads + economy + NPCs + interiors named as one backlog, not four.
items.evidence: Born 2026-06-22: design/showreel-teaser.md ('for the larger game'), the big-game backlog (per-cart work + cross-cutting seams), interiors-brief Round 2, the streetlevel-content stub; the clip pipeline (make-gif clips-carry-audio + compose-clips reels) the same day; facegen's conversation system; a v0 reel cut from 5 carts (sloop/interiors/dialogue/thecut/moog).

items.title: The scrutiny turned from the corpus to the canvas
items.body: Observation 9 was the project building a mirror to read its own CORPUS. The last two days of the window turn the exact same scrutiny one level down — onto the rendering itself. The question stops being 'what can it draw' and becomes 'is what it draws honest, and is it cheap.' The software-canvas probe answers the cheap half by skipping the GPU entirely: rasterize the whole frame on the CPU and prove the result byte-identical to the old path (cityplan 11.4ms→5.0ms). The det-probes answer the honest half: rotated fills, strokes and sprites are gap-free and — once libm trig is quantized — identical across architectures, which is the property the GPU was quietly NOT giving. And the same correctness machinery shows up as graphics gates: road-check and mirror-diff are framebuffer oracles that assert pixel invariants headless, the visual twins of week 3's audio gates and observation 9's spec() harness. It's the third domain to grow a guard rail — audio, then game logic, now pixels — always after the thing it guards already exists, never before.
items.evidence: 2026-06-24: software-canvas Phase 0 GO (cityplan 11.4ms→5.0ms, byte-identical, ADR-flagged GO); det-probes rotfill/rotline/rotspr/textrot with cross-arch libm-trig quantization; road-check.js born 06-24 + mirror-diff.js born 06-23 (both golden-framebuffer oracles); the 'perf' + 'gfx' subsystems each pick up new milestones the same day.

items.title: The roads thread finally grew a city to drive through
items.body: Observation 7 named the roads work as the project's longest unresolved thread — 'started small and would not stop.' June 23–24 is where it stops being just roads. Three city sims wake up on top of the street layer in two days: gridcity simulates the city as feedback (land value ↔ crime ↔ pollution ↔ power ↔ water ↔ fire ↔ RCI demand), cityplan plans it (procedural lots and building interiors as one town), trackgen makes it live (traffic with right-of-way, crossings, K-turns, police chases), and cityview lets you look at it in pseudo-3D. This is the convergence observation 10 predicted — the strands assembling into one artifact — but it's also load-bearing for observation 8's 'second project': a city you can simulate, plan, drive and view is exactly the world the production/economy sims need to sit inside. The honest footnote: overpass was born, lived a few hours as a standalone flyover sandbox, and got folded into cityview the same day — the corpus grows by merging as much as by adding.
items.evidence: Born 06-23: gridcity (SimCity data-layer sim), cityplan (lots + interiors merge). Born 06-24: cityview (four-mode pseudo-3D), linecompare; overpass born-and-deleted (folded into cityview). trackgen traffic-AI Phases A–C (cross-roads, right-of-way reservations, chases) across 06-23/06-24; field-based road rendering + road-check + ADR 0021 (geometry in 2D, view is an adapter) on 06-24.

items.title: Audio stopped being the majority
items.body: For three weeks the revealed identity was 'a synthesizer with a screen' (observation 1) — audio was the majority of everything the project shipped. The back half of the window is where that ends, and not because audio slowed: the instrument racks of 07-02 are some of the richest sound work in the whole repo. It ends because everything ELSE accelerated at once — a real city to drive, the engine running on a phone, a way out to the store. The synthesizer got a screen, then the screen grew a city, a device and a storefront around it, and the sound became one thread among several instead of the trunk.
items.evidence: Audio's milestone share falls from 63% through week 3 (38 of 60) to 33% across the full window (58 of 174); by the last several eras the largest threads are 'store', 'roads', 'ios' and 'mobile', not sound. Yet the instrument work itself never stopped — acidrack, tracker, yachtrack, pocketbox, delia and otafamily are all born on a single day, 07-02.

items.title: The project grew a way OUT — a distribution layer
items.body: Every prior week made THINGS — engines, carts, sims, tools, guard-rails. The last week is the first that builds a way to RELEASE them. de_switch_cart gives each cart its own audio/video/sprite-sheet context so many run in one binary; build-app.js turns a manifest into a multi-cart app; the same manifest builds a signed+notarized Mac .app and runs on iOS with in-app purchases; and a whole ASO/store/press pipeline (research → compose → score → screenshots → press page) sits around the build. This is a new KIND of work again — not engine, not content, not tool, not the guard-rails or the mirror of observations 9/12 — it's distribution. And it obeys the same law as every layer before it: it trails the thing it distributes. You only build an app pipeline once there are hundreds of carts worth shipping.
items.evidence: The distribution cluster debuts across the window's last two weeks — ios (10 milestones), store (7), netcode (5) and the new promote (4), 26 together, 06-29→07-07; ADR-0023 (ship carts as apps, not the editor; 06-29), ADR-0026 (store pipeline in-house, not Fastlane; 07-03), ADR-0027 (sound state via the request queue; 07-03); the umbrella app gets its name, 'Tiny Jam' (07-03).

items.title: The mirror got a second day — and became a habit
items.body: Observation 9 caught the project building a mirror to read its own corpus — almost all in one day, 06-22. Six days later it did it again, bigger, and this time the one-off scripts became permanent EDITOR surfaces. build-context gathers a single cart's scattered docs; its horizontal twin topic-brief does the same for a whole feature; lint-xrefs finds the missing backlinks. Then the views: the ★ design board and the ★ reflections page, both reading a shared doc-status.js that owns the lifecycle vocabulary. And the closing move was pure honesty — an audit of all 15 field notes recording what each actually became, flipping the journal from 10 observed / 0 incorporated to 10 incorporated. The introspection layer stopped being a burst and became infrastructure the editor carries.
items.evidence: 'introspect' grows from 5 milestones (06-22) to 9 — adding build-context, topic-brief + lint-xrefs (432 missing backlinks → 42), the board + reflections pages, and the field-notes audit, all 06-30 — now tied for 4th-largest of 25 subsystems (with gfx); the ★ design board + ★ reflections ship as editor Docs-tab views the same day.

items.title: A guard-rail decision got reversed by making the thing cheap
items.body: ADR-0024 (06-29) settled the on-device renderer: the software canvas is canonical for 2D, and 3D stays off the initial iOS list — because textured triangles measured ~10fps on the phone. That reads like a permanent limit. It lasted six days. On 07-02 a single optimization — sw_tritex's scan-box clamp and hoisted plot — made textured triangles hundreds of times cheaper, and on re-measurement 3D held ~59fps on the same class of device, overturning the ADR's exception. The lesson isn't 'the decision was wrong'; it's that a guard-rail written against a measured cost is only as fixed as the cost. The rail wasn't relaxed — the thing it fenced off got fast enough to let back in.
items.evidence: ADR-0024 puts 3D off the iOS list after ~10fps tritex (06-29); sw_tritex clamp + hoist lands 07-02 (infiniminer 1204→3.4ms, 358×; podracer 89→6ms) and the on-device re-measurement (infiniminer 10.9ms @59fps on an A13) overturns the exception the same day.

items.title: The corpus learned to reshape itself to the glass
items.body: For most of the window each week ADDED to the corpus — a new engine, a new sim, a new instrument. Week 6's dominant thread does the opposite: it makes what's already built fit any device. The framebuffer moves to the heap and grows on demand (byte-identical, so nothing renders differently), iOS carts fill the screen with safe-area insets and rotation, and a run of the densest UIs in the repo — acidrack, acidwire, epianofit, scalegrid — are rebuilt to reflow, the practice then frozen into a responsive-instrument-ui playbook and ADR-0028's sensible-defaults rule. It's the same law as the mirror (obs 9/16) and the guard-rails (obs 12/17): a general capability (device-adaptive layout) only precipitates once there's a big corpus to apply it across, and it trails that corpus. A whole week spent not adding but ADAPTING.
items.evidence: The 'mobile' subsystem reaches 7 milestones (growable framebuffer, the ui-audit responsive-layout gate, acidwire, responsive-instrument-ui, …), all 07-01→07-07; the change ships NO new engine primitive — the growable framebuffer is byte-identical to the fixed one; device-adaptive-layout is the largest active handoff lane (6 docs — see the frontier band).

items.title: Shipping wasn't enough — it built the demand side too
items.body: Observation 14 caught the project growing a way OUT — distribution, getting a cart to the store. Week 6 builds the OTHER half of the funnel: getting it found once there. The trailer builder turns a manifest into a demand-generating video; asc-push marks promoted purchases so IAPs surface on the product page and in search; the leads marketeer maps each cart to its tribe and the venues where that tribe gathers; and the editor's information architecture resolves the whole workflow into Make / Promote / Ship — promotion elevated to a first-class verb with its own per-cart tab, bracketed by Ship, with Dream as the zeroth verb closing the loop to a cycle. The project stopped assuming good work sells itself.
items.evidence: A new 'promote' subsystem debuts at 4 milestones — the trailer builder (07-04), the leads marketeer (07-07), promoted IAPs via asc-push --promote + Store.swift PurchaseIntent (07-07), and Make/Promote/Ship (07-07); leads tunes music-tribe coverage to ~90%; the editor gains a per-cart Promote tab (design/promote-tab.md, 07-07).

items.title: The longest thread started generating its own cities
items.body: Observations 7 and 13 tracked roads as the thread that 'would not stop' and then 'grew a city to drive through' — but those roads were hand-placed or pulled from OpenStreetMap. Week 6 turns the corner from DRAWING roads to GROWING them: a rung ladder climbs four steps in a single day, generating a realistic street network from a population-density field and per-city tensor-field arterials, extracted into a shared world spine (worldnet.h) the moment a second cart consumed it (obs 5's crystallized-duplication law again). And it's fenced by a NUMBER: sndi-check scores a generated network against real cities on a street-network-disparity composite. That makes it the fourth domain to grow a deterministic oracle — after audio, game logic, and pixels (obs 12) — now network realism; and true to obs 12's rule, the oracle (rung 0) shipped before the generator it judges.
items.evidence: worldgen rungs 0–3 all land 2026-07-06 — sndi-check (rung 0), worldnet.h (rung 1), citygrow's density field + tensor arterials (rungs 2–3); 'roads' is the 2nd-largest subsystem at 24 milestones; sndi-check is the 4th deterministic oracle after the audio gates, spec(), and road-check/mirror-diff; first generated-vs-real numbers measured (citygrow mean degree 2.65 vs Amersfoort's 2.71).

items.title: A LAN prototype became shipped web multiplayer in one week
items.body: Multiplayer is another thread only legible in aggregate (obs 7's shape). It began as lockstep over UDP confined to a LAN (07-02); week 6 carried it all the way to the open web in two topologies within days of each other — a deployed relay with cart-scoped rooms and a site play-together button (rung 5a), then a relay-free WebRTC peer-to-peer path at ~12ms (rung 5b), built, play-tested and published. A LAN-only prototype became a shipped, hosted, two-topology feature in a single week — the closing, not the opening, of a multi-week investigation.
items.evidence: netcode grows to 5 milestones; rung 5a relay deployed + wss-verified 07-06, rung 5b WebRTC P2P built + published 07-07; the WebRTC spike measured Mac↔iPhone at ~12ms over wifi (07-06).
